# Exodus 8: The Hardening That God Permits

What strikes you most forcefully in this chapter is not the escalation of plagues—though they are revolting, multiplying, inescapable—but the pattern that emerges with terrifying clarity. Pharaoh breaks his word. Again and again. He promises to release Israel when the frogs infest his palace, when gnats cover the land, when flies swarm across Egypt. Each time Moses prays, God removes the plague. Each time, Pharaoh's heart hardens. This is not weakness on God's part or failure in the negotiation. This is judgment unfolding exactly as the Lord predicted. God told Moses at the beginning that Pharaoh would refuse, that the Lord Himself would harden Pharaoh's heart so that His signs and wonders would multiply in Egypt and His name would be proclaimed throughout the earth. What we witness here is the machinery of that judgment in operation. Pharaoh is not a helpless victim of divine coercion; he is a man who, having glimpsed the power of the true God, chooses his own will over submission. And God, in His justice, permits him to do exactly that—over and over—so that the true nature of his rebellion becomes unmistakable and the power of God becomes undeniable.

Notice too the limit God places on His judgment even as it intensifies. When the flies come, God draws a distinction between Goshen, where Israel lives, and the rest of Egypt. The land is ruined everywhere except where His people dwell. This is not cruelty masquerading as justice; this is God demonstrating His sovereignty over creation and His faithfulness to those He has called. He can plague an entire nation and shelter His people in the same land. He can show mercy to those who trust Him while unleashing judgment on those who resist Him. Every plague carries within it both a message and an opportunity—repent and be spared, or harden yourself and be destroyed. But Pharaoh does neither fully. He negotiates. He makes deals. He appears to consent, then renews his defiance the moment relief comes. This is what spiritual self-deception looks like: the belief that you can bargain with God, that you can partially surrender, that you can maintain control while acknowledging His power.

What is most convicting is Moses' own integrity in this negotiation. When Pharaoh offers compromise—worship your God here in Egypt—Moses refuses. He understands that true worship cannot happen under Pharaoh's conditions or in Pharaoh's land. The sacrifices required by God would be detestable to the Egyptians. You cannot serve the true God while remaining in bondage to human opinion and false gods. Moses will not accept a reduced, domesticated version of obedience. He demands the full three-day journey into the wilderness. He will not pray for relief unless Pharaoh agrees to the complete release. This is the faith that stands against the tide of compromise: knowing who God is, knowing what He demands, and refusing to settle for less even when the power to demand more rests in another's hands. And what does God do? He confirms Moses' faithfulness by removing the very plague that makes Pharaoh's "generous" offer seem reasonable. The Lord will not let His servant negotiate with injustice.

Yet the chapter ends where it began—with Pharaoh's refusal. The plagues will continue. The hardening will deepen. But here is what you must absorb: God's patience with Pharaoh is not weakness, and His judgment is not cruelty. It is the unfolding of a plan written before Egypt existed, designed to display the Lord's power to all creation, to deliver His covenant people, and to show that resistance to God leads only to greater ruin. The frogs, the gnats, the flies—they are terrible, yes, but they are also merciful invitations to submission that Pharaoh repeatedly rejects. What will you do when God speaks?
